title: Excluding weekends due date from start date
excerpt:
Hi, 
I’d like to set a due date from start date + number of hour in effort and exclude the weekends. 
I create the formula to add effort to the start date but I need to exclude the weekends.

posts.author: gbourelly (Guillaume Bourelly)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi,

I’d like to set a due date from start date + number of hour in effort and exclude the weekends.
I create the formula to add effort to the start date but I need to exclude the weekends.

image702×182 15.9 KB

posts.author: mark.anley (Green Llamas Consulting)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @gbourelly - I think you should be able to complete this using the WORKDAY function. Basically if you do the following you should get the desired result:

FORMAT_DATE(WORKDAY({Start date},({Effort (h)} / 7), “YYYY-MM-DD”)

I used an example of February 15 as the start date with an effort of 36h as you have above. It gave me a due date of February 22.

Hopefully this is what you were looking for and feel free to reach out any time.

Mark

Green Llamas

monday.com + Green Llamas

Green Llamas is here to help you make all your People and Projects Green. Let us get your herds under control using the monday.com platform.

posts.author: PolishedGeek (Polished Geek | Platinum Partner)
posts.cookedHtml: Only thing to keep in mind is that Formula Dates are not “real” dates that can be used to trigger notifications. As long as you don’t need to have those dates trigger anything, then formula-driven dates work beautifully as Green Llamas shared.

posts.author: mark.anley (Green Llamas Consulting)
posts.cookedHtml: Excellent @gbourelly - glad this is what you were looking for.

posts.author: mark.anley (Green Llamas Consulting)
posts.cookedHtml: Excellent point! Thanks for bringing this forward @PolishedGeek

posts.author: gbourelly (Guillaume Bourelly)
posts.cookedHtml:
@PolishedGeek , thank you.
That’s why I use the General Caster Integration to cast the result formula in a date column.
